explanation:
The requirement states that if the system exceeds sensor limits, and the pilot is not in control (not standby), and the system is supported without failures (supported and not apfail), then an autopilot pullup should be latched (i.e., eventually pullup becomes true and stays true, as 'latching' typically means a set-and-hold behavior). In LTL, this can be formalized by stating that globally, whenever these three conditions hold (limits & !standby & supported & !apfail), it is required that, from this point onward, pullup is held true forever (this captures the 'latch' behavior). This property correctly encodes the specified dependencies and latching behavior.

Previous LTL formulas may have failed to properly enforce latching (requiring pullup to remain true after the triggering event), or may not have accurately defined the conjunction of the enabling conditions. This property directly relates the specified triggering conditions to the required persistent pullup output using a release (R) or an implication to a global condition on pullup.

The requirement is that if the system reports a sensor limit violation (limits is true), then, provided the pilot is not in control (not standby), the system is supported (supported is true), and there are no failures (not apfail), this must cause a latch of the autopilot pullup (i.e., pullup becomes true) and stays true thereafter. To express that 'latch' behavior, we use that whenever these conditions are met, globally it must be the case that once pullup becomes true, it stays true until the conditions no longer hold. This is most concisely and faithfully captured in LTL as follows:

Whenever (limits & !standby & supported & !apfail) becomes true, eventually pullup should be true, and while (limits & !standby & supported & !apfail) remains true, pullup should not become false. We express that as: whenever the triggering conditions hold, (pullup U !(limits & !standby & supported & !apfail)).

Alternatively, this can also be captured as: globally, whenever (limits & !standby & supported & !apfail) holds, pullup holds too (i.e., G((limits & !standby & supported & !apfail) -> pullup)).

The former is more precise about "latch", but the latter is simpler and matches the natural language if latch is interpreted as 'pullup remains true whenever the conditions hold'. This encoding best captures the input intent over more permissive or looser properties (such as those lacking the persistent requirement).

The requirement states that when sensor limits are exceeded, an autopilot pullup state shall be latched if: (1) the pilot is not in control (not standby), (2) the system is supported (supported is true), and (3) there are no failures (not apfail). In LTL, this means that whenever limits is true (sensor exceeded), standby is false (pilot not in control), supported is true, and apfail is false, then in this situation, the system must ensure that pullup becomes true and remains true (latched). This can be represented by a global property: G((limits & !standby & supported & !apfail) -> F(pullup)), asserting that in all states, if those preconditions are met, then eventually pullup must become true (and, since 'latch' is used, if we want to strictly enforce that pullup must stay true once triggered in such a condition, we may refine to: G((limits & !standby & supported & !apfail) -> G(pullup)), but typically 'eventually' is the minimum latching behavior).

The requirement states that whenever the sensor limits are exceeded ('limits' is true), and the pilot is *not* in control ('!standby'), and the system is healthy and supported ('supported' is true and 'apfail' is false), then the autopilot should latch a pullup ('pullup' becomes true). This means that under these conditions, if 'limits' occurs, then 'pullup' must eventually be true. The LTL below encodes: globally, if 'limits' holds and the pilot is not in control and system is supported and no apfail, then eventually 'pullup' must be true.

The natural language specifies that when the sensor exceeds its limits, the autopilot must latch into a pullup state, but only when three conditions hold: (1) the pilot is not in control (i.e., 'standby' is false), (2) the system is 'supported' (true), and (3) a failure ('apfail') is not present (i.e., 'apfail' is false). 'Latch' in this context means that once 'pullup' becomes true under these conditions, it must remain true. This is captured using the release operator (or until construct), enforcing that once triggered, 'pullup' will remain active while the condition persists. The formula asserts that globally, whenever ('limits' is true and 'not standby' and 'supported' and 'not apfail'), then eventually (F) 'pullup' must become true and stay true as long as those conditions remain. Previous LTLs might miss the latching aspect or might not gate the latch properly by all three conditions.

The requirement states that if sensor limits are exceeded, AND the pilot is not in control (i.e., NOT standby), AND the system is supported (supported is true), AND there are no failures (apfail is false), THEN the autopilot shall latch a pullup (i.e., output pullup must eventually become true and stay true). In LTL, we can express this as: globally, if limits is true, and NOT standby, and supported, and NOT apfail, then from that point onwards, pullup must hold globally (i.e., 'latch' means once the condition triggers, pullup should stay true forever). This is captured as: G((limits & !standby & supported & !apfail) -> G(pullup)). This precisely captures the described latching behavior and the use of the provided atomic propositions matches the natural language requirements; previous attempts might not account for the persistent latching effect when the condition is met.

The requirement states that whenever the sensor limits are exceeded (limits is true), if the pilot is not in control (not standby) and the system is supported (supported is true) and there are no failures present (not apfail), then the autopilot pullup (pullup) shall latch (i.e., become true and remain so, at least for some duration of interest). In LTL, 'latch' is typically interpreted as 'once enabled under the condition, the effect must occur'. So, for all times, if limits is true, standby is false, supported is true, and apfail is false, then eventually pullup must become true. However, to guard against transiently losing the latch, we may want to say that once these conditions are true and pullup becomes true, then pullup must remain true as long as those conditions persist. But as a basic, high-likelihood direct translation, we state that globally, whenever (limits & !standby & supported & !apfail), then eventually pullup. Thus: G ((limits & !standby & supported & !apfail) -> F pullup)

The property specifies that if the sensor limits are exceeded, and the pilot is not in control (not standby), and the system is supported (supported is true), and there are no failures (not apfail), then an autopilot pullup (pullup) should become latched (eventually always true after these conditions hold). The LTL formula enforces that whenever these enabling conditions are met globally, it will eventually and persistently (latch) result in pullup being true. The chosen translation best reflects the requirement of a 'latch' ("pullup" should remain true from the first point the conditions are met as long as those conditions continue to hold). Previous LTL properties might miss the requirement to latchedly maintain pullup or neglect some of the required enabling conditions.

The requirement states: if the system detects sensor limit exceedance, and the pilot is not in control (not standby), and the system is supported (supported is true), and there is no failure (not apfail), then a latch should occur for the autopilot pullup (autopilot must remain in pullup state after being activated by these conditions).

In LTL terms:
- 'limits' should be true (sensor exceeds limits)
- 'standby' should be false (pilot is not in control)
- 'supported' should be true (system is healthy)
- 'apfail' should be false (no failures)
- Result: 'pullup' should be latched (once entered due to these conditions, it should stay true)

This can be captured by stating that, whenever (limits & !standby & supported & !apfail) occurs, 'pullup' must become true and remain true at least as long as those conditions persist (i.e., the latching effect is until the trigger conditions are not present anymore). This is done most simply by requiring that globally, whenever the trigger occurs, eventually pullup holds:

G((limits & !standby & supported & !apfail) -> F pullup)

This means that if at any point, all conditions occur, 'pullup' will eventually be true (latched), as per the description. Edge case (not using U or more complex forms): If a strong latching behavior is required (e.g., pullup stays true as long as the triggering condition remains), an alternative would use U, but this simpler form is the starting point.

No previous LTL formulas provided, but the above logic faithfully captures the stated requirement, ensuring latching of the autopilot pullup as specified.

The requirement states that if a sensor exceeds its limits ("limits" is true), and the pilot is not in control ("standby" is false), and the system is supported ("supported" is true) without failures ("apfail" is false), then an autopilot pullup must be latched ("pullup" is true). In LTL, this can be captured as: Whenever all these conditions are met, then globally (always when these conjunctions hold), pullup should also be true. The 'latch' wording implies sustained activation for at least as long as the conditions persist, which is captured using the release (V) operator: Once "limits & !standby & supported & !apfail" becomes true, "pullup" is asserted and remains so as long as the condition holds. This ensures the pullup remains latched unless the condition is broken. Previous LTL properties may not capture all conjuncts or may not persistently latch pullup for as long as the condition is true; this form precisely covers the requirements outlined.
